skill.name: convex-migration-helper
skill.description: Helps plan and run Convex schema and data migrations with the widen-migrate-narrow workflow, migration component guidance, and safety checks for existing data.
skill.summary: Plan safer Convex schema and data migrations without breaking deployments.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many code execution, weak cryptography, sensitive file, network, and combined-risk alerts. Review found these are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, Convex schema prose, TypeScript migration examples, and documented CLI commands; no credential access, obfuscation, malicious network call, or prompt injection was found. The remaining risk is legitimate but meaningful because the skill guides users through commands and database migrations that can change production data.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate Commands Can Affect Production Data
medium_findings.description: The skill documents npm and npx Convex commands, including migration runs, status checks, cancellation, deployment chaining, and production execution. These commands are expected for a migration helper, but they can modify deployed Convex projects if an agent runs them without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and semantically relevant to the skill. Risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands are standard Convex workflows and do not include user-controlled shell interpolation.

medium_findings.title: Migration Examples Modify or Delete Database Documents
medium_findings.description: The reference examples use ctx.db.patch, ctx.db.insert, and ctx.db.delete for backfills, table splits, and orphan cleanup. This is normal migration behavior, but incorrect use could cause data loss or inconsistent production state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data-changing calls are visible in migration examples and can affect real Convex data. The surrounding documentation recommends dry runs and staged deployments, so this is an expected but elevated operational risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts point to metadata and prose about Convex schema migration, not cryptographic algorithms or security primitives. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptography usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss descriptions, schema design, and type safety. They do not contain cryptographic API calls or weak algorithm names.

low_findings.title: Sensitive SQLite File Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file alerts correspond to Convex database example code using ctx.db operations, not SQLite database files or embedded data dumps. No evidence found of .sqlite, .db, or local database artifacts in the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner appears to have matched db-related identifiers in TypeScript examples. The file content is Markdown documentation and does not include SQLite files or secrets.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs link to the public Convex migrations component documentation. They do not send data, retrieve remote instructions, or exfiltrate credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only URLs found are Markdown documentation links to convex.dev. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or network-capable script in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined-Risk Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined code execution, network, and credential access signals, but review found documented CLI commands, documentation links, and no credential access. No evidence found of obfuscation, secret harvesting, or exfiltration intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context explains the static signals as legitimate migration documentation. Targeted review found no process.env access, token handling, encoded payloads, or suspicious external endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Migration Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include Convex commands that can run migrations or deploy with a chained command. Marketplace users should require explicit confirmation before an agent runs these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real and can affect a Convex deployment. It is not suspicious by itself because it matches the stated migration purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Delete Example
dangerous_patterns.description: One migration pattern shows deleting orphaned documents. This is valid cleanup guidance, but it deserves review because mistakes in predicates can remove data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The delete call is directly visible in a migration example. The context is legitimate orphan cleanup, so the risk is accidental data loss rather than malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Convex schema changes can fail when existing data does not match the new shape. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan staged migrations, backfills, dry runs, and verification steps.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when a Convex schema change needs a migration.
Plans widen-migrate-narrow deployment sequences for breaking changes.
Uses @convex-dev/migrations patterns for batched and resumable backfills.
Provides examples for adding fields, deleting fields, changing types, and splitting tables.
Includes dry-run, status monitoring, cancellation, and verification guidance.
Highlights common migration pitfalls such as premature deletes and large collect calls.
limitations: It does not inspect a live Convex deployment unless the connected AI tool has project access.
It cannot guarantee migration correctness without reviewing the actual schema and data model.
It focuses on Convex projects and is not a general database migration framework.
Production commands still require human approval and environment-specific safeguards.

use_cases.title: Add Required Fields Safely
use_cases.description: Plan a staged rollout that adds an optional field, backfills existing documents, verifies completion, and then makes the field required.

use_cases.title: Refactor Existing Data Shapes
use_cases.description: Move from legacy fields or nested objects to a new structure while keeping reads and writes compatible during the migration window.

use_cases.title: Recover From Schema Deploy Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose why a Convex deploy rejected a schema change and create a safer multi-deploy migration path.

prompt_templates.title: Check Migration Need
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Convex schema change and tell me if it needs a migration. Explain the existing data risk and the safest next step.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Required Field Rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add a required field to an existing Convex table. Create a widen-migrate-narrow plan with deploy steps, backfill logic, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Design Zero-Downtime Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a zero-downtime Convex migration from this old data shape to this new shape. Include dual-read or dual-write decisions and rollback notes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Migration Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Convex migration code for safety. Check batching, idempotency, dry-run behavior, production command risks, and final schema narrowing.

output_examples.input: I need to make role required on users, but existing documents do not have role.
output: The skill would recommend adding role as optional first.
It would then backfill existing users with a default role in batches.
After verification, it would narrow the schema to make role required.

output_examples.input: I want to replace isPro with a plan field on teams.
output: The skill would suggest keeping both fields during the transition.
It would map existing boolean values to plan values during migration.
It would delay removing isPro until reads, writes, and verification are complete.

output_examples.input: My Convex deploy fails after removing a field from schema.
output: The skill would identify existing documents as the likely blocker.
It would recommend marking the field optional, removing stored values, and then deploying the narrower schema.

best_practices: Start with a widened schema that accepts both old and new data shapes.
Use dry runs and status checks before changing production data.
Verify all documents are migrated before narrowing the schema.
anti_patterns: Do not make a field required before existing documents have that field.
Do not use collect on large tables when batched migrations are available.
Do not delete legacy data until all reads, writes, and rollback needs are resolved.

faq.question: Does this skill run migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides plans, examples, and command guidance. Any production command should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Which projects is it designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for Convex applications that use TypeScript schemas and Convex functions.

faq.question: What is widen-migrate-narrow?
faq.answer: It means accepting old and new data first, migrating stored documents, then requiring only the new shape.

faq.question: Can it help with zero-downtime changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes dual-read and dual-write strategies for changes that must stay online.

faq.question: Does it replace testing?
faq.answer: No. Dry runs, development testing, backups, and production review are still required.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it documents commands and database changes that can affect production data.
